Let me start this by saying that I first purchased this app back about midway through COVID. I was super excited and wanted to workout in some way despite gyms being closed. Fast forward a year later, I KNEW that my body needed to get active and that I could achieve my goals and more with this app if I was in a good space mentally. So, when I was, this time around I was going to use this app to its fullest extent. You can customize this app in every way possible. From the type of food (vegetarian, vegan, pescatarian, gluten free, dairy free, etc) to the types of workouts. Styles such as boxing, MMA, yoga, Pilates, HIT, and MORE. Since recommitting to my health journey, I have genuinely fallen in love with eating delicious food and working the heck out. This app also provides articles that are credibly sourced. There are also a ton of other golden nuggets in this app. I absolutely this app, thank you Chris Hemsworth and his team for the heart and soul put into this. Also, I’m a huge marvel fan so when I saw that I could basically get in shape like Thor - I couldn’t refuse. I mean who wouldn’t want to get jacked like that legend!? GET THE APP :)

I love this app! I’m a long time Daylio user and I was very excited to see what they did with a food journal app. 

As someone who struggled with an eating disorder most of her life, I find a lot of “calorie tracking” or “nutritional tracking” apps to be highly triggering. I don’t always want to see a number on what I ate, I don’t want to measure out my portions and get notifications that a food I entered was “too high in saturated fats”. Apps like these are what threw me down the rabbit hole to begin with, allowing me to sink deeper in my disorder. 

My favorite thing about Nutrilio is how customizable and judgement free it is. I have the option to put in just what I ate and how it made me feel and define what I felt to be healthy for me, instead of feeling shamed everything I have a bowl of Cheetos. It gives me an opportunity to develop a healthier relationship with food and how I eat in a very eating disorder recovery- friendly way. At the same time, it allows other users to add in calories if that’s something they wish to track, along with things such as exercise and water intake.  I think this app is genius because of how adaptive it is. 

If you’re familiar with Daylio, you’ll be pleased to know they kept the same fun, colorful theme they have in their other app. The bright color scheme and goal achievements they incorporate always create such a positive experience for me, which I think is so important when you’re using apps like these to better your mental health. 

The only downside, for me, is unlike Daylio where you can make a one time purchase on the app to unlock premium content- Nutrilio offers a monthly or annual subscription to get additional content. The subscription isn’t that expensive, but I’m unsure if it’s something I want to pay for at the moment. Hopefully that will change with whatever updates they bring in the future.

I love the capability and genuine health benefits of the Cardiogram feature. Full disclosure, I worked part time as a data scope technician on a coronary rehab floor in a major hospital while attending Iowa State University. This was decades ago when subjecting a patient to any level of exercise while recovering from a heart attack was regarded as a dangerous idea. There were a few cardiologists, however, who thought this should be challenged and carefully designed progressively increasing exercise activity for capable patients on the heart floor. This was found to be an effective form of rehabilitation and has become standard practice in coronary care. 

The summer this was being setup in the rehab wing of the hospital, I was working in the surgery post ops recovery room. I was invited to join the team of techs that were being trained to monitor the cardiac rhythms of patients in the units who were, in these pre-wireless days, connected 24/7 to large, heavy monitors at their bedside and grouped in the nurse’s station. 

We technicians underwent weeks of training to recognize and record the ecg rhythms of every patient. We were given state of the art instruction about all the possible heart rhythms and had to learn how to recognize irregularities, record them and take immediate action if they were life threatening. I am forever grateful to the doctors, nurses and hospital for that experience. 

Fast forward decades to what we now wear on our wrists and you can understand how some of what this watch can do seems like science fiction. It’s fun to look at my rhythm and consider the p-waves, qrs interval, t wave and all the possible aberrancy's I learned and still can recall all those years ago. So, you see, mine is probably not an independent, objective opinion. The fact that this consumer product can display a certifiably accurate rhythm pattern not only amazes me as a breakthrough for science and cardiac monitoring. It’s an incredible tool for the safety and advance warning of the wearer’s heart rhythm and will undoubtedly preserve and save lives of all who use it.

Loved using the app from the start.  I’ve used several and this is my favourite.  Got suckered in by great marketing and a man crush for a certain mr C.Hemsworth but stayed and paid because it delivers on what it promises

Positives 
- Very simple to use
- Looks very slick
- Huge variety in workouts
- Tailored to individual goals
- easy to cook and tasty meals.
- bonus features they introduce you to. Eg the wellness/mediation and the stretching (I’m guilty of waddling out the gym early before this app) 
- informative blog section. 

Negatives/feedback 
- can’t record the weights you lift. Some find it too much admin but it’s nice to see progression and keep those notes in the app. EDIT after using the app for a few weeks you have such a variety of workouts it doesn’t seem practical to input weights every single time, and with a change of mindset I’m now quite happy to just quickly assess weights before a workout as I’d much rather have the variety than a 16 week program doing the same exercises each week. 
- Website crashes occasionally, saying “oops something went wrong”.  Only fixed when I logged out and in again. 
- it didn’t remember I’d paid an annual fee (has now been fixed through the app by clicking “restore purchases”). Coincided with the day my annual subscription fee came out
- music inside app can’t be linked to services other than Apple Music. Not that big an issue as you just play Spotify/soundcloud etc in background
- sound cutting out. When I have music from other app playing in background and a timer goes off eg end of a stretch session, it doesn’t play the music again, until the 3 second countdown as it introduces the next exercise/stretch. It then cuts out again as the exercise/stretch fills the screen.

I have a personal reason for using Cardiogram.  I had an acute MI almost 20 years ago.  That event damaged my heart’s internal pacer.  Subsequently, I often suffer dysthymia...  poor internal control of my heart rate.  My heart runs slow...  my bradycardia is in the 50’s and goes as slow as the 30’s during sleep.  As you might guess, I have an embedded pacemaker.

Having a heart make random transit into error modes like A-Fib and Bigeminy is rather scary.  I am thankful to this app’s developer(s) for their commitment to measuring one’s heart pace continually.  I find the recorded results helpful to authenticate my heart’s excursions to my Cardiologist.

I find myself ignorant of the method to chronicle my heart’s data via Cardiogram.  I want to give my cardiologist a general idea of the ‘roller coaster’ ride my heart beat changes cause during times awake and asleep.

If you have any doubts why you might be feeling uncomfortable or nauseous, or you suspect your heart is slow or racing, this tool will testify to the events a heart clinician should be made aware of.  It is a ‘wake up call’.

Cardiogram is a simple comprehensive tool.  As of it’s latest release version, as a cardiac patient and a retired software engineer, I am impressed with it.

Give it a try...

Having been in a bit of a rut with my workout lately (lift 3 days and run 3 days), I decided to try out Centr, the new app by Chris Hemsworth and his team.

I downloaded it, and set my profile. The guidance was if you train 5-7 days a week, then go to "advanced". 

So I set my workout for "Chris Hemsworth" level. 

I downloaded Chris's personal playlist to go along. I saw AC/DC. Eminem and Imagine Dragons, realized Chris and I are basically brothers and figured this should be fun!

I started my workout...

40 seconds later, I was questioning every decision I have ever made....

2 minutes in, the dog came to check on my like "Boss, we good?"

4 minutes in, I'm pretty sure vultures were circling my house.

5 minutes in I realized, "that's just the warm up !?!?!?"

Kept going

10 minutes in, I'm yelling at Mr. Hemsworth like "Oh, Mr. "Fancypants"! You are just so "In shape" Yes, I can do chinups...I can't do A MILLION OF THEM!"

20 minutes in, and I'm pretty sure I saw a Joel-shaped hole forming in the universe

Finally, I saw those two magic words..."workout complete"

While I was collapsed on the floor, I decided to look up Chris's recommended amount of alcoholic intake (for a friend)

Hemsworth recommends two drinks...A WEEK!?!?!?!

I went back to yelling at Mr. Fancypants

Seriously though, this is a great app. Love the workouts, the recipes and the holistic approach to fitness.

No music. No clock. No guidance. No list of upcoming exercises. No instructor energy. The exercise videos are just not at a level you would expect from a purchased app. There’s no clock to tell you how much time is left, just a meaningless bar at the bottom of the screen. The instructors don’t provide suggestions to improve technique. There aren’t additional people in the videos showing modifications. This format is certainly not for beginners, as they’ll quickly become frustrated. The lack of music in the videos contribute to the overall lack of energy.  The lack of ‘workout buddies’ in the videos also make for a poor video. There’s no opportunity for the instructor to show good form, modifications, or even banter. What you end up with is a very flat, uninspiring workout video. There isn’t anything special about the routines. Pretty standard stuff. Compare this to the energy, excitement, and variety you get in a Les Mills or Beachbody workout, and you’ll instantly understand what I’m talking about. 

I tried this, because programs like Les Mills lack the overall integrated diet and wellness plan, but those features can’t overcome what the Centr exercise routines are lacking.

I am a nurse and a very healthy person that contracted Covid 19 at work.  I did not know that I had the virus except for the fact that my vision was different and I was urinating more frequently.  These changes occurred over a one month period so I didn’t think much about it.  When I did go to the doctor I was told that I was positive for the virus and that my blood glucose was 455.  I didn’t feel sick no fever or cold like symptoms, just elevated blood glucose.. I stayed in the ICU room for about 2 days and then I went to a regular room…I was given insulin around the clock to return my glucose to a safe range, 250 before I was released…..Then my journey began, I am now diabetic…eating healthy, drinking more water, and most importantly, walking daily… I couldn’t have lost 49 pounds and monitored my vitals signs, and made all the necessary changes without the information provided to me by this app.  It is my first line of defense, knowing.  Knowing my numbers, heart rate, and being able to target my goals.. yes it is a great app.

I don’t think I’ve ever written a review on an app but I had to take the time to say how much I’ve been enjoying using this one. And yes it is 100% free. I love that all of the features it advertises stay true to being free. There is an option for some features that require in app purchases but it is all for extra things to enhance your experience if you want them. You won’t hit a paywall using the free version. This lets you track your meals briefly or in detail with the option to add a note. You can customize your supplements and medicine that you take and it tells you exactly what time you took then which is so helpful. You can set a water/liquid intake goal and you can add it per amount of ounces you drink and it adds it up for you. It even takes the water percentage out of the other beverages that you drink and puts it towards your hydration goal. You can set personalized reminder notifications as well which are super helpful. You can also track health symptoms which you can also customize. I love that everything is customizable and it gives you little stickers to choose from too. You can track your weight and exercise daily as well. This has helped me stay on track with my diet, vitamins, medications, etc. It is so quick and convenient. Literally the only thing I wish it would do that it doesn’t is timestamp when you enter your meals. I always just put the time I ate in the notes section. But yeah if you’re looking for a food journal or tracking app this has it all. It’s extremely user friendly and aesthetically pleasing to look at too. 5 stars!

I take a whole laundry list of meds that change frequently, and I just found out I'm autistic, and I have been battling major depression, PTSD, and anxiety for years. I also have chronic migraines from a severe head injury. Because of my autism, it's been difficult to communicate how I'm doing to my therapists and psychiatrists and doctors, and often I was not believed about the severity of my symptoms, or accused of not taking my medication, or told that my sleep or energy is better than I think it is. Now I have EMPIRICAL DATA all gathered in one place that provides an accurate assessment of my moods, symptoms both mental and physical, everything all in one place. My only complaint is that I wish you could time stamp EVERYTHING, not just medication times and mood and energy but also factors and symptoms instead of being limited to 4 time ranges because I'd like to see if a short-term intervention works and right now I can only see that I listen to relaxing music when I get anxious, but not whether I feel less anxious after listening to relaxing music. But this is the best app out there right now.

This is an incredibly good app. It really integrates all the aspects of wellness that we all want to/need to work on in one very attractive space. COMPREHENSIVE: Centr incorporates workouts for fitness, meal plans and recipes for nutrition, meditation for mental and emotional stability, sleep visualizations for better sleep, plus regular articles on bettering your relationships, reducing your stress, learning to eat more plant-based meals, proper nutrition, allergies, etc. CUSTOMIZABLE: Centr adapts everything to you. Enter in your age, current fitness level, goals, dietary guidelines and preferences, and your planner will only include the workouts, meals, and special features tailored to you, spaced throughout the week for optimal balance. Change those guidelines at any time, or if a specific recommendation doesn’t work for you (like you’re still too sore for that kind of workout on that day, or you hate broccoli), you can easily do a one-time swap. And if you have an Apple Watch, the app will automatically record stats like your time, heart rate, and calories burned whenever you start a workout. THOUGHTFUL: Centr’s production value is really high; this feels like a high-quality, well-designed product. The team’s professionals are both knowledgeable and personable, the locations are often stunning, sound and video quality are high-def, graphics are crisp and clear, and the food photography is magazine-quality. Design touches like the subtle yellow progress bar at the bottom of the screen during your workouts or the way the video moves from black-&-white for a couple of demo reps to color when you’re meant to start working, show that a lot of care and thought went into the development. PERSONABLE: The team is consistently encouraging, and infuses their workouts, blogs, articles, and special posts with personality. Every Friday there’s a special video of bonus content featuring different coaches, Centr users, or Chris Hemsworth giving tips, encouragement, accolades, or just a bit of fun to make you feel more connected and happy about your journey. I know – it’s such a glowing review that it sounds like I work for them somehow. I don’t, but I am incredibly pleased with the app. I initially balked at the price of the service, but after a couple of weeks of using it, I cancelled my membership at my Pilates club, which cost me more for 4-sessions-a-month membership than Centr costs for an entire year of daily everything, so it has streamlined my life, too. Centr has quickly ended up taking the place of my gym and my workout videos, all my cookbooks and my shopping list app, my sleep sounds and meditation apps, and even my health and wellness magazines and blogs. And with new programs and trainers constantly updating the service, I never get bored. If they ever stop doing what they’re doing, I’m screwed, but for now, I’m very happy.

I got this app because I’d heard it was good for managing chronic illness. And it very much is! Bearable has already helped me make some correlations that have improved my quality of life— things I wouldn’t have noticed on my own. 

But within this mission lie several functionalities that I was previously using multiple apps to attend to. (And it was becoming unwieldy! There comes a point when you’re spending more time setting up and updating apps than actually living your life, especially if you have some sort of executive dysfunction.) Bearable offers a multitude of functions that other apps offer as their *sole* function (and charge large monthly fees for).

And it is so entirely customizable that you can track all of your health issues in its single interface. Sleep patterns, pain, GI issues, energy, cognition, mood, allergies, heart issues, menstruation or menopause, weight loss, body measurements, hydration, dietary habits and suspected troublesome foods, exercise, autoimmune or eczema flares— anything you want to track, you can, along with potential influencing factors (again, customizable). And if it has a corollary in Apple Health, you can have the entries sync with each other. 

I also really appreciate the optional inclusion of what many other health apps leave out— social and lifestyle factors such as being outdoors (and the weather), time spent with friends/ partner/ family, screen time and video games, alcohol and caffeine intake, and any custom habits you suspect could be having an effect on your overall wellbeing. 

The yearly subscription fee is totally reasonable for this all-in-one bundle of functionality. There is a dedicated subreddit where the developer participates and is very responsive to feedback and suggestions, and where the enthusiastic user base makes it clear that this app has improved the health and lives of countless people. I’m so grateful that I found Bearable.

In the process of trying to tend to multiple overlapping short- and long- term physical and mental health matters, including a challenging diagnostic path for a possible rare genetic condition, I realized that keeping a notes file with general observations wasn’t cutting it anymore. It became abundantly clear that I’m going to need much more granular data to not only help parse my complicated presentation, but also to advocate for myself with providers and insurance. I researched multiple health and lifestyle tracking apps before landing on Bearable and giving it a trial week. I didn’t need a week, though — It was clear on the same day that I downloaded it that it was a keeper. 

Bearable offers a flexible system for tracking not just symptoms but a whole range of lifestyle factors that influence wellbeing. Its default set-up is pretty robust to start with and may serve many users well without much tweaking. That said, it was super easy and intuitive to use its defaults as a jumping off point for adding custom factors to track (i.e. very specific symptoms such as left hand tingling/numbness vs right, and daily activities specific to my life, like how much VR headset use). 

After you get a few days logged, the app starts to provide correlations between the tracked items. While correlation isn’t causation, seeing these connections offers plenty of insight to allow for greater self-awareness and to help guide inquiries to providers. In under a week it has given me new awareness of some semi-regular activities that appear to positively impact my mental health, so now I can explore the connection intentionally. Never would have put two and two together on my own. 

Perhaps the best part is discovering how much the (small!) Bearable team works with its users to make further developments. Multiple platforms exist to interact with the community of Bearable users and the creators, where requests and ideas are shared and bounced back and forth with the creators as they make developments. I’ve never seen a company walk the talk SO thoroughly on their commitment to being user driven. 

This might be a weirdly long and effusive review , but it’s impacted me that much.

Thank you so much, Bearable team. Your app, and your palpable care, have given me a practice tool *and* a dash of empowerment and optimism about my own health.

I’ve been using this religiously for about two weeks and have lost about 6 lbs.  it is the best (only) meal planner app I have found and for the most part it works very well once you get the hang of things.  

Some things I like:
- Most basic foods are preloaded with calorie/nutrition information.  This allows you to use the database to easily craft your own recipes knowing exactly what you’re putting in them. 
- Many of the recipes on the app are very good.  I have at found least 10 new recipes that are very healthy and that I will continue to make for the rest of my life. I’m taking about you “spicy tuna and cottage cheese” lunch. Lol
-Adding custom foods is easy with only basic nutrients (cal, fat, carbs, protein) needed which is easy to find and enter
-The amount of customization/personalization is great, allowing you to relatively easily construct your own recipes. 
-The best part.  It’s super easy to modify portions to meet your calorie goals.  Following the portion amounts teaches you healthy habits that will last as long as you let them. 

Room for improvement:
-The app generated meal plans are not great.  Sometimes it will pair 2 main dishes for a meal or give you very weird combinations.  I use it as a starter but modify pretty much every meal. 
-For some reason when you personalize a recipe it drops the image.  If it’s basically the same meal with slightly altered portions or a substitute/extra ingredient it would be nice if the same picture could stay up. Also, it is a little clunky to try to add your own image.  This is a small quip but I like looking at pictures of what I’m gonna eat. 

Overall you have to know what you’re getting yourself into when following a meal plan.  It takes time, planning and work.  With that being said I think if you can commit to investing the effort this app will definitely help you be successful.  I’m keeping it until I hit my goal weight and then will probably discontinue but still use the recipes and use what I learned about ingredients and portion sizes to maintain (hopefully) permanent healthy weight.

This app is very dynamic, and it’s getting more and more so. You have many options of ways to handle tracking your health. The devs are kind and helpful and so interactive with the community. They ask for feedback on designs and ideas and listen when people ask for certain features. 

This is the best health tracking app I’ve come across in my years of searching. 

As a minor suggestion for the devs:
Since your app’s strength is how many ways it can be used, there are some little changes that would increase that greatly. 

- Allow custom rating systems for symptoms and other things. (e.g 1-10/3-46 or whatever other number scale versus a type in option versus an emoji option etc). You already have all of these features but only certain features can use it (only heath measurements can use type in, only symptoms can use 1-4 etc). If all the features that it makes sense for could be customized as to what they’re using the app would be much more dynamic. 
- Allow us to customize which features we use. (Think strategies/features from Amazing Marvin). This would be become more useful as you add more things so it doesn’t clutter the homepage of the people who don’t care for certain features. 

This one is more difficult but I think it would help a lot as I know you get complaints about the homepage setup sometimes:

-Allow us to further customize our homepage. I would like to be able to group things together that aren’t necessarily from the same feature. (e.g. put a health measurement and a factor in the same group, or a symptom and a medication etc. rather than having all factors together and all custom ratings together) This would allow us to create a custom process for logging as well as a design that we personally like.

Let me start off by saying I think it’s a great app. I am leaving the four stars because I think with some simple updates most of my critiques could be fixed, and the main portion of the app, the workouts, are wonderfully balanced and give you so many options. The meal plans on here are great because they repeat things throughout the week so you don’t have to buy a million different ingredients each week, and they taste amazing and have so many different cultural options which I really have enjoyed. The centr power workouts are great if you have done weightlifting before and I love the mixup between trainers and techniques. However, I feel there are some somewhat major issues with some of the main components of the idea to use this as a whole rounded daily planner. First, the programs that you are able to choose based on your entry level and goals are not automatically added to your planner. Instead you access them through the specific program section of the app while it still plans other workouts for you in your planner. I do not understand why you would offer options but then not implement them in suggested workouts when we are telling you what we are trying to accomplish. Along with this, the suggested modifications for some of the exercises are great except for the fact that they are very hard to see as they can only be viewed in a small corner of your screen because there is no way to choose it to make it larger so you can actually see the movements. Also, the meal plans are really good, like spectacular actually, but the serving sizes on a lot of options are just outrageous. I just made the Green Super Smoothie and with the directions it made 8 cups on a single serving setting. I do not know in what world I need like four full smoothie cups and am still supposed to have snacks in between the other three planned meals. There should be some way that they consider your height and either current weight and goal weight to better determine calorie and macro needs because I feel like they’re made for like a 250lb 6’4 guy. Again, good base, but needs some more fine tuning to be worth the $120 year long subscription.

My favorite app. I set it for "Intermediate" and "Build Muscle" and it gives me the only workout regimen that I've ever been able to stick with. I actually workout 7 days a week now. As of last week, I hadn't had a rest day in 4 months. I hadn't needed one. The workouts actually leave you with enough energy to do the other workouts for the week. That's not to say the workouts aren't tiring (Da Rulk's workouts in particular just annihilate me). You're gonna be exhausted by the end of the workouts if you're doing them right, but you'll be able to get up the next day and keep at it. It genuinely feels like magic. Now, I haven't been using the meditation or the nutrition portion for awhile with a few exceptions. The beef udon noodle and chicken stir fry recipes are absolutely out of this world, and have become weekly staples in my family's diet (because we all love them so much, nevermind that they're healthy). I haven't tried many of the recipes, but that's just because when it comes to food, I like to stick with things that I know I'm going to like. I'm not a big fan of trying new things in general. I have abs now. I've never had abs before this app. I'm more fit than I've ever been (in strength, speed, and flexibility), I feel absolutely amazing, I love the variety, and I'm always excited to hop in the gym. I even get compliments on my physique every now and again. My main fitness goal was to find something that I could stick with for life. I can finally say that I've met my main fitness goal with this app. I always recommend this app to anyone who will listen to me gush about it. I consider it my golden ticket to lifelong fitness, and I genuinely think it could be everyone's if they give it a fair chance. 

 EDIT: I had to take off a star because there were some changes made that I dislike... a lot... to the point that I felt like taking a star away. I got sick and hadn't been on the app in awhile. Now that I'm back, I noticed that the weekend workouts are easier. The weekend workouts were fine honestly. I'd even say that all of Luke's workouts were perfect without going overboard. Now these new workouts apparently don't have a self-guided option to them and force you to replace the workout entirely with a different one. I don't like coached workouts bc I want to listen to my own music. And I don't like hearing unnecessary things like "keep it up!" That might sound silly to you, but it makes me angry bc I know the coach can't see me, so it doesn't encourage me, it just makes me lose focus for no reason. Keep in mind that I made no changes to the app whatsoever. Still build muscle/intermediate. Just suddenly have coached workouts on saturdays out of nowhere that are easier, and I just don't like that change. I hate having to manually pick which workout I'm gonna do on saturday now too bc I'll always feel like I'm picking the wrong one. I also apparently got put in some program randomly awhile back that I didn't sign up for, so I quickly quit that one. I get wanting to push a new workout regimen, but keep your customers in mind. Maintaining any exercise routine can sometimes be a very fragile thing. If I find one that works and I'm actually, miraculously able to stick to it and continue making progress, please don't force change, especially if it's towards a goal that I'm not after. Still a great app, just starting to dislike the direction they're going.

Edit again: So they essentially acted like they implemented something new with swapping workouts between coached and self-guided workouts, but that's always been there. The thing I have a problem with is that the specific workouts don't mean anything to me. I don't know what I SHOULD be hitting really, so I don't know how to CHOOSE a workout, you know what I mean? It'd be immensly helpful to me if there was like a "suggested swap" or something. Not a "Here's every single workout we have. Pick one" sort of thing. I like having that option, I just don't like not knowing which would be the "best" replacement. I'm also still not a fan of coached workouts and I wish I could turn them off completely without having to replace them constantly.

I just started my free trial and I love the app! It’s so well designed, and the Planner feature is so helpful!  

Just one thing - I’m 70 years old with arthritis, balance and mobility challenges, and the workouts are too hard for me. Even the beginner level workouts go too fast for me and have jumping/ crossover exercises that cause my joints to hurt during and after the session. 

I realize I’m probably not your primary target demographic, but I used to be and I want to recover the mindset and active lifestyle I used to enjoy. 

Is there a way I can insert my own physical therapy exercise workouts and walking sessions to the Planner?

Also, is there any chance you would consider adding some Boomer level programs with joint-friendly workouts that incorporate pt exercises for knee, hip, and lower back strengthening, as well as progressive plans  for cardiac conditioning?

I realize that you’d have to tell us to follow our doctor’s prescribed exercise recommendations, but basic accredited programs are available from places like the American Physical Therapy Association, the Arthritis Foundation’s Walk with Ease plan and AHA’s Walking 101.  Could you add something similar to those? It would be so great to be able to plan those in Cntr and not have to go to a bunch of different places!

I’m guessing there’s a product manager that you’d need to talk to about this. Would you kindly present my case?  Boomers are old, but we’re not all dead, and lots of us are not ready to be in walkers. We are overall an active generation. Some of us just ruined our knees and hips dancing at Grateful Dead concerts. Also, your current target demographic will someday be our age. Wouldn’t it be cool to help them transition to an active lifestyle in their later years?

A few weeks ago I was talking with a coworker about fitness (both very serious about it) and I happened to mention that if there was a body type I am trying to achieve, it would be like Hemsworth’s (who wouldn’t?). I got home, saw an ad for the app, laughed to myself, and immediately signed up as a founder and waited anxiously for the launch out of curiosity.

I did the first exercise yesterday, which was the chest and back burner by Torre Washington. I liked that it was easily adaptable to the small apartment gym I had access to at the time. It was a nice workout, definitely started to fatigue a little bit (unfortunately, partly because of a bug I will mention shortly), although I took it lighter than I could since it was a new routine. 

The only real issue I had was that the app would skip the rest period between sets. It would show in the list, but skip right over. What was said to last 50 minutes only ended up taking ~30 minutes. Now, since it was reps, I could have just rested the minute anyway, but I could see that being problematic in some cases, such as with timed routines.

Also, while I didn’t go super heavy, I was a little surprised at what was considered advanced. I like workouts to really kick my butt. Another level of intensity would be nice for people who have been doing this for a while but want new ideas. But that is just one workout so we’ll see.

Otherwise, the recipes look great so far and some of them are almost what I typically eat anyway, which is reassuring but also helps gives ideas to mix it up.

I hope the app keeps up with these first impressions and stays as one of my top fitness apps.

Not good enough for being the most expensive app for training. I've been using the app for about a month now and I have to say it didn't meet my expectations. I was looking for an advanced, all-around training assistant and given the hefty price tag, the trainers and some of the reviews, I thought this might be the one. Some technical complaints: - If you switch to another app in the middle of a session (even during rest) the timer stops working, the same happens when you turn off the screen and the app may even close after a while - The interface for logging the weights is clunky, you have to traverse a few 'touches' before getting to it About the programs: - I chose a Centr advanced program and I've been following it, however it doesn't seem to integrate to my daily routine because the app keeps showing other workout for 'daily routine'. - The recipes and great, however there's no weekly planner and only a meager shopping list - The customization is not nearly as half as other similar apps Despite this I think it is a good app, the programs are great and the trainers are first class; it just feel like an incomplete product for being the most expensive traing app on the market.

All of the other reviews I’ve seen saying the privacy policy is intrusive very much understate the case. 

This thing wants *all* kinds of personal data - and makes it clear that even if you use a burner email or FB account that you aren’t safe because they actually track your Apple Watch’s serial number and ownership data, among other things!

They say they need all of this do they can send you update notices, allegedly not for marketing purposes. 

Furthermore, they won’t even remove your data even if you delete your account - or even if you ask them to do so!

If you live in California, and only then, you can request information about what data they have on you, but they still won’t remove it for any reason. 

They do make clear that they are not subject to HIPAA, so forget that protection, too. 

The video shows an actual EKG tracing, but there is no evidence in the preview data that that is actually part of the app as downloaded. 

They don’t even offer an actual monitoring service that would notify you if there’s a problem with your heart that is detected in exchange for all of the personal data. 

Deleted without opening - and I recommend you don’t even download it.

I’m currently on the 14 day trial and will likely continue with the annual subscription. As someone who desires to be fit and healthy but is also too busy to plan everything in advance on my own, the app is a game changer. I love the level of customization allowed (although this is a double-edged sword which I’ll get to later). I can manually enter exact calories and macronutrients either to the gram or % of calories. I can eliminate foods I either don’t like or can’t eat and they will never be generated in the plans. 

Why 4 stars and not 5?

While the app is just about fully customizable, the interface isn’t the most intuitive and so the more you choose to modify and specify the more work and thought you will have to put in upfront. You may wonder, as I did, if the app is actually saving you time or not. However if you do it right the first time, you shouldn’t have to re-create it again. 

Another caveat is that my wife and I don’t have the same nutrition and food preferences. So, planning for us both has proven difficult. There are workarounds, but they will add another level to the time and work you need to get the app working the way you want it.

I was really excited to try this app when I found out about it last month because like most people I’ve always wanted to know what it is that celebrities eat or train that gets them into shape so quickly for movies and stuff. The idea for the app is genius and I think their motives are in the right place. 

It’s finally released and I hate to say it but I’m extremely disappointed. My rating has nothing to do with some of the bugs which I’m sure they’ll take care of. (A major one I found was nutritional info that was obviously wrong). 

The reason why I rated the app 2 stars is because it’s not really automatic and guided as they make it to be. For example when you want to swap out an item they don’t automatically suggest other meals that are within the same nutritional content they tell you to look and make sure you swap correctly. Also 2 meals for my week were listed as “eat leftovers”! If we’re paying for an app to tell us what to eat it’s a ripoff to suggest leftovers because everyone already knows that’s an option for any meal. Another factor with the meals is they have things like whole chickens as a meal and other things that seem much more difficult to cook or take a longer time than most people will have the time to do. But then on the same realm they do things like suggest using microwave brown rice which cuts that recipes time but not everybody likes to eat rice that’s microwaved. So I feel like there is a disconnect with the audience there.

I like that they have so many work out options but again when dealing swapping the app doesn't suggest similar workouts to keep you on your goal. Also I was disappointed that you cannot swap a single exercise within a workout. Additionally, I wish there was a way to tell the app how many days a week you wanna workout because it has you going everyday. 

Overall, if this was a $5/month app I wouldn't be as critical but as a premium app that promises a personalized experience with Chris Hemsworth's team it doesn't offer anything over other apps that do the same for a lot less.

 It's not a BP cuff by any means , a helpful tool for someone like myself with PTSD to check actual numbers helps me stay calm, and reassures me that I'm OK.

The ability to have a meal plan built for you based off macros using realistic meals (with dietary and allergy restrictions available) that you can adjust to a price budget is an incredible addition to my IIFYM lifestyle!! Saving me HOURS of planning meals and try to adjust the macros to suit the day. 

The only downside is the food database and the difficulty personalizing meals and foods. For example, counting macros is important, but it’s equally important to be flexible. If the serving size says “1 medium banana” and counts the macros for 118g of banana, but your medium size banana is a little short (96g). I personally would like to be able to adjust the grams to see if it’s really worth cutting up another banana, but in order to adjust them on the app you would have to essentially create a new basic food or custom food for a 96g banana. Also, the database for custom branded foods is pretty small. Lastly, if I add a recurring food to my plan but adjust it, there’s no way to adjust throughout the weeks making you have to manually adjust it. 

Overall, worth the cost and all the little hiccups so you don’t have to pull your hair out counting macros in a self-made meal plan.

First of all, this is the best workout and health/fitness app on the App Store. It has a great variety of workouts, excellent recipes that are healthy and easy to make, and has a holistic wellbeing approach to emotional and mental health that other apps lack. 

That said, it’s missing a few core features that would make it a 5 star app:
1. The ability to save favourite workouts and swap them in as required. Sometimes I want to do a particular weight set because I know the next day I’ll have to just do bodyweight exercises. Let the users save favorites and customize their plan a bit!
2. Save favourite recipes. My wife is gluten free but I’m not, so when I find a good gluten free recipe I want to save it. I can pull it from the website, but why add extra steps? Let us save the recipe list in the app for easy access. 
3. An iPad app. I workout without glasses on and it’s tough to see the screen sometimes. I can use the iPad but it is awkward just using a 2x sized phone app. I would love an actual iPad app to use as a companion to the phone app. 
3. When playing the workouts, indicate what the next exercise will be during the rest screen. Right now I have to pause it and get setup which throws off the flow of the workout. If the next workout was displayed earlier I could flow between exercises more smoothly.

I also haven’t had to go to the store for any items I forgot or ran out of. I love the meditation add on’s now that I’m a quaranteacher. I am a penny pincher and never spend money on apps, but I’ll be buying this one for sure. With the grocery savings I actually think I’m SAVING money. I am on the second of the 6 week long centr6 workout. Overall, this app has a lot of potential but I wouldn’t recommend it. 

1) Workouts: the workouts run three trainers showing beginner, intermediate and advanced movements at the same time which is pretty cool. The biggest issue on this is the frequency of mistakes where intermediate and advanced end up doing the same thing is excessive. Second, you cannot tell the application what equipment you have. Finally, the workouts are pre-recorded where they do 1 set of 6 and the remaining 5 are minor poor voiceovers. 

2) App support: not responsive, I messaged them on the first week using the app and on week two I’ve not yet received a response.

3) Planner: not very straight forward to use and frustrating at times with a big lack of customization. 

4) Meals: honestly, probably the best feature of the app, the meals are very good, high quality and easy to do 

5) Chris: you barely get to see him using the app, it’s more like a marketing tool to attract you but his involvement seems very minor.

I think that this app has the ability to become one of the elite workout/fitness apps that are available.  It has a wide variety of workout regiments, calming meditation practices, and healthy recipes to make it an app that is second to none. But there are issues with its tech interface that really hold the app back from being what it can be. First, the connection with the Apple Watch. The system is very clunky on its connection to the watch. I will be finished with a workout and then look at my watch to see that it’s still going. I then have to go into my watch and end the app there, which prevents me to record all the data from my workout (Time working, calories burned, etc.). Also, if your gym doesn’t have good reception, you can’t see the workout if you are watching through the video selection. I wish that the app would allow you to download the workout to your phone so that you can see what needs to be done without having to rely on cellular reception. Next thing, I think the app should allow you to see upcoming segments of the workout while it’s paused. It’s a little bothersome that you have to let the workout out play through while you are looking at what comes next (This is mainly for timed workouts). Then having to go back to the workout you were on because it went to the next one while you were looking at what comes next (I know that a little thing but it would be a nice touch in my opinion). Finally, I feel like the app would be great if it added a feature where all different types of workouts are stored in a library (Individual workouts. Not the full workout sets). For someone that might not want to follow the videos, and use an assortment of other workouts from different workout videos, would be fantastic. This app has great potential, and I hope that it can work on the technological issues it has, but at the time, I don’t think this app is for me. Well, for now at least.

Meals need a planning aspect to them so you can see all your meals a week in advance so you can tally up ingredients needed to make efficient use of trips to the grocery store.

Daily workouts and meals seem random - they lack consistency with stated fitness goals. Why pay to get the same random workouts once you have learned them?

*******
For self improvement addicts like myself or anyone struggling with mental or physical health, Bearable is an AWESOME way to record interventions like new meds and supplements and different activities or exercises to see how they affect energy and happiness on average over the long term.

There can be an awfully confusing matrix of variables playing a role in mental (and physical!) health, from social media to sleep to weather to specific places we go daily and people we see, and Bearable lets you track ALL of these. You just type them in and the app works in the background making associations (‘insights’) for you over time and showing you patterns you might not otherwise see.

They have truly thought of everything - and anything else you think of, you can add yourself! Every category is customizable - you can both add options and take away ones you don’t use. 

For instance I added Sugar Cravings to my Symptoms list and was able to see I have Fatigue 100% of the time I have sugar cravings. This is not surprising but it’s a great reminder to see in black and white that when my brain says chocolate, what I really need is a nap.

I haven’t even grazed the real depths of what this app can do in my first month using it and I look forward to exploring more of its capabilities and insights. 

I can’t think of any condition this app wouldn’t be super helpful for - weight control, depression, PMDD, anxiety, addiction recovery, migraines, chronic fatigue - and it’s great at keeping everything in one face with a fun, friendly, motivating user interface. 

I just want to say THANK YOU to the developers. I have dreamed of an app like Bearable for as long as there have been apps. I’ve used various trackers to monitor mood, health factors and habits for years but I can bring everything under one roof now.

This app is fine if not a bit limited in content. But my issue isn’t with the content or design. It’s with their predatory subscription practices. I had used the app off and on since the day it released up until the end of 2020 at which point I unsubscribed completely. Fast forward to April of this year and I randomly get a charge for $119. Me being confused as to why went and redownloaded the app to look and see what happened. To my disbelief I had been resubscribed to the year plan and my card that had been on file was charged. I immediately canceled that subscription, deleted my card and emailed customer support. They responded and said that they do not provide refunds. I explained that this was fraudulently completed and asked for management to look at it and they responded and said that management sees nothing wrong and told me that they can see that I logged into the account a month ago even though I haven’t had the app since last December. They have no sympathy or even the slightest regard for customer service at all. My advice to you: don’t even go down the rabbit hole, there are plenty of other great workout apps and programs out there that don’t do shady things to charge you extra money. However if you do I would recommend removing your card from their site as soon as you set your subscription so they don’t charge you randomly like they did to me. Now I’m out $119 and I will make certain that I turn everyone away from this app, report them to the BBB and any other steps I can take to mitigate this happening to anyone else in the future.

2. The “timeline” is horrible. It shows you practically no data yet is filled with ads to “add friends” and “try premium” except the only additional features available at this premium price are the ability to share data with your family and friends, which is useless for me. I was hoping for additional insights on my personal data yet there is nowhere to find this information. It’s basically a more ad-filled and less-informative version of what I already have on the Fitbit app.

3. Overall this basically works like a diary. You can enter if you were happy, sad, stressed, drinking, etc. during high heart rate periods but that does absolutely nothing because you need to go to that specific day and all you see is the info you put in. So as a result, I'm accidentally swiping and causing it to regenerate the meal. There's no way to confirm or cancel, so I ended up fouling up my meal plan a couple times now. Yes, it is user error, but the UX is less than ideal which is a design problem.  \n\n  UPDATE: Here I am 2 years later and while I was cooking, my screen dimmed. I’m also curious as to how often the app is updated - and I’m not talking about the content because I did notice some new content got added recently. I’m talking about updating  the basic functionalities of the app. Most recently, the workouts that I’ve completed in the Centr Power: Beginner program have not been marking themselves as completed and I’ve also lost the ability to manually mark them as complete on the weekly program pages. I did end up subscribing to a year of Centr because that’s how much I really love the content, but it’s basic things like what I described above that cause me to NOT recommend this app to others. PLEASE take the time to improve the app so that is consumers can continue to benefit from it and improve our overall wellness and our lives.
Also i cannot find anywhere that discloses the pricing for the premium so this makes me want to run the other direction. Just be upfront and direct with your pricing.

I’m fairly new to a complicated chronic illness and learning how to manage it has been really confusing and hard. 

It’s so hard to know what unexpected factors might be exacerbating problems, and the stakes feel really high for drawing connections as fast as possible to avoid further damage. 

I really wanted a way to track EVERYTHING, like every small bit of data I could, to see if sense could be made of it, and was thinking it would be nice if there was an app for it but didn’t feel hopeful about finding something as comprehensive as I was dreaming of.

So I have been delighted over and over again with how comprehensively Bearable has already solved this problem! The fact that it was made by someone with a chronic illness really shows, because this is so thorough and so easy to modify. I’m just using the free version and it’s blowing my mind, I can’t even imagine how much MORE comprehensive it gets with the premium upgrade!

But whoever is in charge of Torre Washington part of the app should get fired, let alone Torre gives most of the sessions, like 5 times a week. The experience Torre is giving is completely different than the other 2. Without Torre this app would easily get a 5. 
1. No sound, no music, just showing GIFs. That’s a worse experience than just YouTube some videos. You can argue you don’t watch videos while doing it, but that really is a “start with picture” job. At least, tell us a little bit what are some key points in this motion. Just a little bit would be better than dead silence. 
2. You have to press next every time you are done with anything. 
3. The only time it has sound, is the count down timer in the last 3 seconds, and it pauses your music.... what? 
4. You only have 3 seconds to see what’s next, what equipment you need and do it already. Are you expecting us to pull out a yoga mat, put down phone, get down and plank all in 3 seconds? 
5. Anything you do, like restart your music, pauses the workout, and the continue button is so small on the bottom corner, extremely hard to press, and can mistake as the iPhone 10 home button, which you know, pauses the workout. 
6. You can’t apple airplay with Torre Washington, but you can with the other 2. So getting the motion right becomes even harder with this tiny screen. Again, please say something. 
7. Torre Washington also do some motion differently between left and right, which one should I go with man? 

So the whole workflow is, pick up phone, press next, see what it is, 3 seconds to get all equipment ready, do it, count down timer pauses music, pause the workout, restarts music, get back to app, continue. 

There was also a bug where I restarted my music the app just stuck on the last set of workout, can’t finish. Had to restart the app, then all progress is lost. Great job Torre Washington Team, you have created something I can only describe as utterly annoying.

While beautifully laid out and with a very slick set of photos, videos and information, it’s not very easy to navigate, making it overly complicated to actually apply that knowledge. Other reviews seem somewhat polarized on this; either overly gushing and positive or lamenting shortfalls. It's unclear which are genuine.

The workout section is the clearest with an easy way to follow the exercises and mark them off as you do them. And there are very clear tutorials. However, there’s limited flexibility; you’re either doing the exercise or you’re not. You can’t trade them in or out if you have an injury or don’t have access to weights etc.. An app like FitBod does a much better job of this.

The meal planner is great for inspiration and the food looks amazing but if you don’t have a personal chef (or you actually have to go to work), you’re going to struggle to follow the meal plan. For example, coconut porridge for breakfast; 60mins prep time, 30mins cook time. How many people have time to spend 1 1/2 hrs. preparing breakfast?

It would be far more useful to know the nutritional goal for the meal or day (protein / carb / fat per meal) and have a way to track against this, with the recipes, which are fantastic, as an inspiration for how to hit that goal (if you have time and the ingredients to do it).

Content looks beautiful and it’s clearly taken a lot of work to build so hoping that it simplifies and fixes these things in time. For the time being, it doesn’t seem to be worthy of the subscription cost.

One trick though if you’re using two devices, which I learned the hard way. If you started on your phone and try switching to an iPad/tablet, you’ll get a warning message to logout on the other device. YOU MUST LOG OUT OF THE OTHER DEVICE WHEN THAT ERROR MESSAGE IS SHOWING. Don’t click OK and logout later; and weirdly, don’t log out EARLY before trying to log in on the new device. Somehow, that error message makes the data sync possible. If you log out of the first device before or after the error screen shows, you’ll lose your work. Great app though!

I've wanted to use this app since it was first launched but hadn't had the funds before now. When I took advantage of the six week trial in March 2020 I was not surprised to find it superb and well thought out. The ability to focus on weight loss, strength, or fitness and the way it shapes your daily planned workouts and meals is intuitive, and the intensity can be changed at any time. I just took on my first programme Unleashed, and I'm looking forward to seeing the impact. Highly recommended.

Like so many people that have reviewed this app, I rarely review things unless they piss me off or I feel like they need to be changed. But this freaking app has managed to do what Noom, Running Trainers, and so many other fitness apps and programs have failed to at a FRACTION of the cost.

You get (relatively) personalized meals with a TON of variety, but with a weekly plan that you can edit so it doesn’t break the bank; workouts that are great for either at home or at the gym (although, for me, it would be great to have the option to switch around the workouts a little since neither my gym nor my house have the equipment for boxing) and they’re so varied that I’m actually excited to open the app in the morning; AND, as if the app couldn’t get any better, you get meditation and sleep visualization that are so calming and relaxing it brings you right back down from a crazy day so you can sleep or switch to a relaxing task.

My only big-ish things I wish they would update are an option for people that don’t eat red meat, but eat seafood and poultry, a “switch” option for the workouts (and potentially a blog or something about how to make workouts more accessible when you can’t lift your body long enough for a military press-up), and either more or repeating sleep visualizations so that you get one in everyday’s schedule rather than once every couple days.

Overall, I LOVE this app. And for only $50/quarter (which, might I add, is 300% cheaper than Noom), I’ll keep loving it for as long as I can.

PROS - Upgrade to premium. Go ahead do it - it will save you so much time planning your grocery shopping. So much time. When you use the leftovers option + count any other family members also eating the same meal it saves on your grocery budget. For us the cost of premium paid for itself with the first shopping trip. It will continue to pay for itself for the rest of the year as I am now spending about 35% less of our monthly budget. The cost per day is great. It’s not super accurate but it gets close enough to save in the wallet if you’re already budgeting. Plus you can tell the app to use up all the food on your list + pantry to eliminate food waste. 

CONS: I wish I had known from the very beginning to go through our pantry and refrigerator and input what we already had BEFORE generating the shopping list. I had shopped before finding this app. I generated a weekly menu that looked good enough and swiped right on everything I had already purchased. Once in the pantry I updated the quantities to what we actually had on hand. Then proceeded to add everything else in our pantry and refrigerator that wasn’t on the list. I then asked the app to generate a new menus and shopping list. BIG mistake. Only after I spent a few hours cursing the app in frustration did I realize that all the staple items we had purchased that I was swiping right on were would disappear every time I went to auto generate a new menu and update the grocery list. Apparently updating the grocery list also means updating any pantry items not manually input by the user. Anything manually added stayed untouched. This was incredibly frustrating to figure out. 
The other big con is no way to rate the recipes. I have cooked and baked for quite a while and know how things should work and why certain things like pancakes and muffins need certain types of ingredients (chemistry) so I’m able to determine when a recipe that skips these ingredients is 100% likely to fail and be a waste of food. Similarly a recipe calling for a ridiculously small amount of a seasoning that should be used as the main flavor is flat out wrong. My wife however did not know this and so we had a few disasters with some recipes. I was able to modify the recipes to keep the macros similar but still actually work (you can’t skip on leavening when it’s needed folks but you can substitute ingredients to make it happen and save calories). 

For the developers this is a good strong app. The only improvements I would do currently is - Improve the walk through/tool tips to let users know how the pantry/grocery list works (see above) and - Offer visible aggregate reviews of recipes so users don’t have to be bakers or chefs to understand what is/isn’t right in a recipe (like 80% that tried this give this a thumbs up or 30% thumbs up or not enough reviews yet)

I’ve learned to review the recipes and modify some of them to fit our tastes/needs/actual leftover ingredients on hand so we eliminate food waste as best we can. I do like that I can do this on a per meal basis and then refresh any remaining meals to hit my macros.

My husband had purchased this app & had been using it for a few months after he suffered a PE.  He recovered well, but wanted to have a way to analyze the data from his Apple Watch in order to monitor his heart health.  Well, a few days ago, his tooth became infected which resulted in a root canal, then the next morning he felt horrid, he opened the app to see that his resting BPM was 178 and he hadn’t done anything.  his heart was in AFIB, so I called the nurse practitioner on the back of our health card who directed him to go to ER Immediately!  Sure enough his heart was in AFIB and they took care of him there.  It was a scary situation, but glad we had the app to clearly display his heart activity, and now takes his heart health more seriously.  When you can actually see what your heart is doing, it makes you want to implement healthier lifestyle choices.  I also like that the app lets you share your information with anyone you choose.  You can printed it out for your doctor also.

I signed up for the trial for this app to see if it would be a good fit for me and my lifestyle. Unfortunately, it did not (and that’s okay, it’s not for everyone). However, my disappointment comes from the customer service teams and the policy of this company.

This free trial comes with a 7-day trial period after which you are charged the TOTAL amount for the plan that you chose when signing up. Through my own fault and miscalculation of “days since signing up”, my 7-day trial expired and I was charged the full amount for one year’s worth of this product that I will not be using.

After realizing the mistake a day or two after the trial expired, I reached out to the customer service team to come up with a solution for either a refund, or partial refund, for the product that I was not going to utilize. The heartless response that I received was, in short, “we do not offer refunds”.

I understand, I made a mistake and should have cancelled prior to the 7-day trial period expiring, but I’m human and made a simple mental error. I offered a solution, saying I was willing to pay for one month ($30) and have them refund the balance. Again I was met with, “we do not offer refunds”. 

I would think that during times like these, especially times like these, where people are down on there luck and having difficulty making ends meet that there would be some sort of sympathy and humanity. However, I was not met with either of these sentiments. Rather, a harsh “no” to my request.

Again, I realize that I am at fault. I just wish that there was a world where there was a human element in business and business transactions. A simple exchange with the customer service department resulting in some sort of positive resolution for both sides is all I was looking for. Instead, I have left the situation adamant that I will not support this app, and by association Chris Hemsworth, all due to a situation that could have been easily solved with even just a shred of sympathy and humanity.

I've struggled with a variety of illness' and symptoms since I was a child. Before Bearable trying to keep track of everything was always difficult and I found myself getting lost in symptoms. 
I've tried other symptom logging apps but they were either too open ended or too rigid on what the symptoms and intensity should be and neither matched up to what I needed. Normally I have trouble sticking with things but I got the email today that i'm 30 days on bearable, right after logging my symptoms into the app! 
Im very grateful for bearable because now all of that chaos is behind me. I can easily log my symptoms, nutrition, water, sleep and more with a few pushes of buttons. I love how customizable the app is, you can add or take away common symptoms and rate the severity of discomfort. 
I also appreciate how the data is organized that's collected, I can easily see what my symptoms have been for each category organized by date. This has greatly helped with doctors appointments and remembering exactly when symptoms began or were difficult, intensity, and other factors from that time.
100% recommend this for anyone struggling with chronic illness

These alerts come even though I have opened and am using my exercise app on Apple Watch 4 which syncs to cardiogram. Cardiogram app recognizes that I am in a workout as when I finish my workout by selecting end workout I get a nice workout display on the cardiogram app. I shouldn’t be receiving high heart rate alerts during a workout as I am trying to reach my target heart rate which is almost 150 bpm. I joined Centr to start the year to try something new for home workouts. I don’t use the meal portion of the app, and while it seems basic at a glance, I can’t offer any insight into its effectiveness. 

That said, the programming is boring and lazy. The structure and most movements are the same week to week. All of the movements are pre-recorded individually (with few exceptions) and offer no coach engagement. I would like to see coaches do the workout as prescribed so we can see their strain and gauge what our effort level should be at a given time. I don’t need Peloton level coach engagement, but I’d also prefer to know that these strength coaches believe in the workouts they’re programming enough to do them (the HIIT guys seem to do the full workouts more often).

There is no % of a 1RM offered to help guide what weights to use in workouts, nor individual workout guidelines to use (just a vague write up they republish every Monday). 

The app is designed very basically, and I feel like there should be a place to enter the weights after your workout so you can easily reference those in the future when you see the movements again. I don’t like that if I’m using my phone and switch to a different app, the rest timer stops. Sometimes I want to change the music or respond to a message during the programmed rest and I wish I didn’t have to guess how long I’m out of the app for.  

Overall it’s an extremely basic workout regimen. I missed my cancel date for my trial and so I’m trying to use it periodically and see if it improves, but 10 weeks in and it’s still all the same. Hopefully they innovate and advance the workouts or else it will be an easy decision for most to find a better app.

I’ve scoured the appstore and tried numerous apps for health and symptom tracking - I can’t find anything better than Bearable. The dev is responsive and releases frequent tweaks, fixes and upgrades. Privacy is a cornerstone - if you think about it, you probably DON’T want an app scraping your most personal info and selling it to the highest bidder, right? Bearable is free to download but to really use it isn’t free - most apps these days aren’t - but the subscription is at least reasonable. Some similar apps do MUCH less and cost MUCH more. (Gyroscope, I’m looking at YOU.) Nothing is really free, but hey, I like to be paid for my work and I assume devs do as well. FWIW, I loathe subscriptions, but Bearable is one of the very few that I renew with no hesitation. Bearable is comprehensive and flexible - you can add or remove almost anything and track it according to frequency, intensity and quantity. You can hide stuff but keep it in the database - for example, if your allergies tend to flare up in the spring and fall but not the rest of the year, you can hide the entry from the main input screen and limit the clutter. I haven’t really figured out the data manipulation part yet - the app apparently does all kinds of data gymnastics, but I haven’t wrapped my head around that part. Bearable is unfortunately currently only available for iPhone. I’m not as young as I used to be - my eyes aren’t the best and I have sausages for fingers. find the small screen clumsy and unfriendly for graphs, charts and comparisons. I’d really like to see a native (landscape!) iPad app that would either sync via iCloud or allow data export/import from iPhone. Finally, while you can manually enter weather data, it would also be great to have the app automatically sync local temp, cloud cover, humidity, etc. in the same way that Bearable can already collect blood pressure, weight and such from HealthKit. Bottom line: it’s closer to perfect than any other app I’ve tried in this category or any other tracker I’ve tried to wrangle into working.

There are many people who bought the Apple Watch intending to use this FREE app to monitor their various heart conditions. Many of those people cannot afford a subscription because they didn't budget for that. If they had known that before buying the Apple Watch, they may have opted to buy a different watch product completely. The company response mentioned having to shift to a subscription-only model due to costs associated with maintaining an app, costs of data centers to store the data, costs of engineering staff to build new functions, and costs of an expanded customer support team. I'd be curious to know how many data centers are actually needed, and how many engineers there are. It would also be interesting to see how many people are in customer support and how that number changes over the next year. I think this app has a lot of good things going for it. But, I think the people who were already accessing the app for FREE should be “grandfathered in.”
